pick.rationale: Illinois is one of the strongest 3-seeds in the bracket, ranking 6th-7th nationally with the #1 adjusted offense in the country (AdjO 131.2) and a 24th-ranked defense, producing a staggering adjusted efficiency margin of +32.14 compared to Penn's +0.8 at 159th in KenPom. The 24.5-point spread reflects the enormous quality gap, as Penn's Ivy League-caliber offense (215th nationally) will be completely overwhelmed by Illinois's elite Big Ten-tested attack, and the Quakers may be without leading scorer Ethan Roberts due to a concussion that sidelined him for the entire Ivy League tournament. While TJ Power's heroic 44-point performance carried Penn to the tournament, replicating that against Illinois's defense is a near-impossible ask, making this one of the most lopsided 3/14 matchups in the bracket.

reasoningStep.title: South 3/14: Illinois vs Penn
reasoningStep.summary: Illinois is a dominant 3-seed with the nation's top offense and a roughly 31-point efficiency margin advantage over an Ivy League 14-seed that may be missing its leading scorer to a concussion.
evidence: Illinois ranks 6th-7th nationally (KenPom/Torvik) with AdjO 131.2 (#1), AdjD 99.1 (#24), and an efficiency margin of +32.14
Penn ranks 153rd-159th with AdjO 106.9 (#215), AdjD 106.1 (#103), and an efficiency margin of +0.8
Penn's leading scorer Ethan Roberts is questionable with a concussion that kept him out of the entire Ivy League tournament
The spread is Illinois -24.5 with -10000 moneyline odds, reflecting one of the most lopsided 3/14 matchups in the bracket
Penn won the Ivy League tournament behind TJ Power's 44-point heroics against Yale, but replicating that against Illinois's defense is extremely unlikely
The ~31-point efficiency gap per 100 possessions far exceeds the typical 3/14 quality differential

result.text: Quakeaways from No. 3 Penn men’s basketball’s Ivy League Tournament final win over No. 3 Yale – Ivy Hoops Online Skip to content Menu About Us On the Vine Inside Ivy Hoops Find Your Ivy Men’s Schedules Women’s Schedules Ivy Hoops Online Home of the Roundball Poets Quakeaways from No. 3 Penn men’s basketball’s Ivy League Tournament final win over No. 3 Yale March 16, 2026 March 15, 2026 by Ian Wenik TJ Power just put up the best individual performance in Quakers history. His 44 points just led Penn to the NCAA Tournament for the first time in eight years. pic.twitter.com/QfnBWFsIlo — Ian Wenik (@IanWenik) March 15, 2026 ITHACA, N.Y. – Penn men’s basketball is headed to the NCAA Tournament for the first time in eight years after outlasting Yale in overtime, 88-84, in one of the greatest games in recent Ivy League history Sunday at Newman Arena. The Quakers (18-11) needed a transcendent performance from forward TJ Power to pull off the Ivy League Tournament final upset with leading scorer Ethan Roberts back in Philadelphia, sidelined with a concussion. Boy, did Power deliver. The junior had a 44-point detonation, which matched Hassan Duncombe for the program’s single-game scoring record since it joined the Ivy League in 1954. Power personally erased what was a four-point Penn deficit with 12 seconds to play by simply dribbling into three-pointers on consecutive possessions. The last of those threes, a contested shot from the right wing, tied the game at 75 with a second to go in regulation. Yale guard Trevor Mullin (who had hit two clutch free throws to extend the lead to three before Power’s heroic shot) nearly sank a three-quarter-court heave as the buzzer sounded, but it clanged off the back iron. In overtime, Power — whose free-throw shooting struggles this season have been well-documented — put the Quakers ahead for good with 3:02 left in the extra session following two makes from the charity stripe. He got a ton of help from senior guard Cam Thrower, who had a five-point scoring burst in a 40-second span to give the Red and Blue some critical breathing room. In his first campaign running his alma mater, coach Fran McCaffery has pulled off one of the biggest single-season turnarounds in recent college basketball memory. What should Penn fans hold onto from an afternoon of unbridled joy? Power is a transcendent talent. Well, duh. There are few transfers this season that have had a bigger impact on their new teams than Power. Recruited over by Jon Scheyer at Duke and set up to fail by Tony Bennett and Ron Sanchez at Virginia, Power has proven his five-star recruiting pedigree out of high school was well-deserved by reuniting with a coach in McCaffery who has believed in him since his AAU days. Yale threw everyone it had at the 6-foot-9 Power. Ivy Player of the Year Nick Townsend. Ivy Defensive Player of the Year Casey Simmons. Mullin. None of it worked. Power got to his spots both inside and o

While it’s not guarantee that he’ll return to the lineup for Thursday's game, the senior will attempt to practice Tuesday. Penn forward Ethan Roberts was sidelined for the Ivy League tournament with a concussion. He plans to practice on Tuesday. Read more Yong Kim / Staff Photographer by Sean McKeown Published March 16, 2026, 9:08 p.m. ET After its shocking upset over Yale in the Ivy League tournament final, Penn may see its senior captain Ethan Roberts return to the lineup ahead of March Madness. On Monday, Quakers head coach Fran McCaffery told the media that Roberts, Penn’s leading scorer, will attempt to practice Tuesday after missing the Ivy League tournament due to a concussion and could return ahead of the Quakers’ first-round matchup. Advertisement Penn will face third-seeded Illinois in the first round of the NCAA Tournament on Thursday at Bon Secours Wellness Arena (9:25 p.m., TNT) in Greenville, S.C. “I think he’s going to try to practice tomorrow,” McCaffery said. “So we’ll see how that goes.” » READ MORE: Penn’s Ethan Roberts is out of the Ivy League tournament due to a concussion Roberts, who is averaging 16.9 points, suffered a previous concussion against Villanova in the Big 5 championship on Dec. 6 and missed six games due to injury. The time spent away, however, didn’t impact his production, as the forward earned All-Ivy second team honors in his final season wearing Red and Blue. AJ Levine, the teams starting point guard and defensive standout, praised his teammate ahead of Roberts’ potential return, saying the 6-foot-5 forward is the hardest player he’s ever had to guard. result.text: Quakeaways from No. 3 Penn men’s basketball’s Ivy League Tournament final win over No. 3 Yale – Ivy Hoops Online Skip to content Menu About Us On the Vine Inside Ivy Hoops Find Your Ivy Men’s Schedules Women’s Schedules Ivy Hoops Online Home of the Roundball Poets Quakeaways from No. 3 Penn men’s basketball’s Ivy League Tournament final win over No. 3 Yale March 16, 2026 March 15, 2026 by Ian Wenik TJ Power just put up the best individual performance in Quakers history. His 44 points just led Penn to the NCAA Tournament for the first time in eight years. pic.twitter.com/QfnBWFsIlo — Ian Wenik (@IanWenik) March 15, 2026 ITHACA, N.Y. – Penn men’s basketball is headed to the NCAA Tournament for the first time in eight years after outlasting Yale in overtime, 88-84, in one of the greatest games in recent Ivy League history Sunday at Newman Arena. The Quakers (18-11) needed a transcendent performance from forward TJ Power to pull off the Ivy League Tournament final upset with leading scorer Ethan Roberts back in Philadelphia, sidelined with a concussion. Boy, did Power deliver. The junior had a 44-point detonation, which matched Hassan Duncombe for the program’s single-game scoring record since it joined the Ivy League in 1954. Power personally erased what was a four-point Penn deficit with 12 seconds to play by simply dribbling into three-pointers on consecutive possessions. The last of those threes, a contested shot from the right wing, tied the game at 75 with a second to go in regulation. Yale guard Trevor Mullin (who had hit two clutch free throws to extend the lead to three before Power’s heroic shot) nearly sank a three-quarter-court heave as the buzzer sounded, but it clanged off the back iron. In overtime, Power — whose free-throw shooting struggles this season have been well-documented — put the Quakers ahead for good with 3:02 left in the extra session following two makes from the charity stripe. He got a ton of help from senior guard Cam Thrower, who had a five-point scoring burst in a 40-second span to give the Red and Blue some critical breathing room. In his first campaign running his alma mater, coach Fran McCaffery has pulled off one of the biggest single-season turnarounds in recent college basketball memory. What should Penn fans hold onto from an afternoon of unbridled joy? Power is a transcendent talent. Well, duh. There are few transfers this season that have had a bigger impact on their new teams than Power. Recruited over by Jon Scheyer at Duke and set up to fail by Tony Bennett and Ron Sanchez at Virginia, Power has proven his five-star recruiting pedigree out of high school was well-deserved by reuniting with a coach in McCaffery who has believed in him since his AAU days. Yale threw everyone it had at the 6-foot-9 Power. Ivy Player of the Year Nick Townsend. Ivy Defensive Player of the Year Casey Simmons. Mullin. None of it worked. Power got to his spots both inside and o

Penn forward Ethan Roberts was sidelined for the Ivy League tournament with a concussion. He plans to practice on Tuesday. Read more Yong Kim / Staff Photographer by Sean McKeown Published March 16, 2026, 9:08 p.m. ET After its shocking upset over Yale in the Ivy League tournament final, Penn may see its senior captain Ethan Roberts return to the lineup ahead of March Madness. On Monday, Quakers head coach Fran McCaffery told the media that Roberts, Penn’s leading scorer, will attempt to practice Tuesday after missing the Ivy League tournament due to a concussion and could return ahead of the Quakers’ first-round matchup. Advertisement Penn will face third-seeded Illinois in the first round of the NCAA Tournament on Thursday at Bon Secours Wellness Arena (9:25 p.m., TNT) in Greenville, S.C. “I think he’s going to try to practice tomorrow,” McCaffery said. “So we’ll see how that goes.” » READ MORE: Penn’s Ethan Roberts is out of the Ivy League tournament due to a concussion Roberts, who is averaging 16.9 points, suffered a previous concussion against Villanova in the Big 5 championship on Dec. 6 and missed six games due to injury. The time spent away, however, didn’t impact his production, as the forward earned All-Ivy second team honors in his final season wearing Red and Blue. AJ Levine, the teams starting point guard and defensive standout, praised his teammate ahead of Roberts’ potential return, saying the 6-foot-5 forward is the hardest player he’s ever had to guard. - **Illinois**: KenPom #7, Torvik #6, 24-8 record, Big Ten. #1 adjusted offense nationally (AdjO 131.2), solid defense (AdjD 98-99, ~24th). Adjusted efficiency margin ~+32.
- **Penn**: KenPom #159, Torvik #153, 17-11 (now 18-11 after Ivy tourney), Ivy League. AdjO ~106-107 (215th-226th), AdjD ~105-106 (103rd). Adjusted efficiency margin ~+0.8.
- **Spread**: Illinois -24.5, moneyline -10000
- **Penn injury**: Leading scorer Ethan Roberts has a concussion, missed the Ivy League tournament. He was "going to try" to practice Tuesday, status uncertain for Thursday's game.
- **Penn's path**: Won Ivy League tournament as 3-seed, beating Yale 88-84 OT behind TJ Power's 44-point performance, without Roberts.
- **Efficiency gap**: ~31 points per 100 possessions — this is an enormous mismatch.
- **Historical context**: 3-seeds beat 14-seeds ~85% of the time, and this gap is wider than typical 3/14 matchups.
